column3: a person or country that starts an argument, fight, or war by attacking first
column4: He claimed that he was just defending himself, and that the other guy was the aggressor.
column5: The nation viewed the neighboring country as the aggressor in the ongoing conflict.
column6: The boxer came out swinging, determined to assert himself as the aggressor in the match.

column3: suddenly filled with strong feelings of shock and worry
column4: When she heard the shocking news, she was left utterly aghast, unable to comprehend what had happened.
column5: She was aghast at the sight of the damage caused by the natural disaster.
column6: Her parents were aghast when they discovered the extent of her injuries from the accident.

column3: someone who does not know, or believes that it is impossible to know, if a god exists
column4: Although he was raised a Catholic, he was an agnostic for most of his adult life.
column5: As an agnostic, she remained open-minded about the existence of a higher power, preferring to focus on living a moral life
column6: The scientist's agnostic stance on the origins of the universe allowed her to explore various theories without bias.

column3: pleasant or pleasing
column4: We spent a most agreeable evening by the river.
column5: She found the idea of a weekend getaway to be quite agreeable
column6: The team reached an agreeable compromise after a lengthy negotiation.

column3: speed and eagerness
column4: She accepted the money with alacrity.
column5: He accepted the challenge with alacrity, eager to demonstrate his skills.
column6: The employee responded to the urgent request with alacrity, completing the task ahead of schedule.

column3: although
column4: The evening was very pleasant, albeit a little quiet.
column5: She decided to accept the job offer, albeit reluctantly, as it required relocating to a different city.
column6: The car was comfortable, albeit somewhat outdated in terms of technology.

column3: quick to see, understand, and act in a particular situation
column4: I'm not feeling very alert today - not enough sleep last night!
column5: The lifeguard remained alert, scanning the pool for any signs of distress.
column6: The security guard's keen alertness prevented a potential break-in at the museum.

column3: to make someone feel that they are different and not part of a group
column4: Disagreements can alienate teenagers from their families.
column5: His abrasive behavior began to alienate his colleagues, causing tension in the workplace.
column6: The politician's controversial remarks threatened to alienate a significant portion of the electorate.

column3: To make quiet or put at rest; to calm
column4: The government is trying to allay public fear and concern about the spread of the disease.
column5: The doctor's reassuring words helped allay the patient's fears about the upcoming surgery.
column6: The company took swift action to allay concerns about the safety of its products after reports of contamination surfaced

column3: to make something bad such as pain or problems less severe
column4: The medicine did nothing to alleviate her discomfort.
column5: The charity's efforts to provide food and shelter aim to alleviate poverty in the community.
column6: The therapist suggested mindfulness exercises to alleviate stress and anxiety.

column3: attractive or exciting
column4: I didn't find the prospect of a house with no electricity very alluring.
column5: The boutique displayed alluring dresses in its window, enticing shoppers to step inside.
column6: The mysterious allure of the ancient ruins beckoned travelers from far and wide to explore their secrets.

column3: something that is said or written that is intended to make you think of a particular thing or person
column4: Her novels are packed with literary allusions.
column5: The novel contained a subtle allusion to Shakespeare's famous tragedy, Romeo and Juliet.
column6: The artist's painting contained an allusion to Greek mythology, with figures resembling ancient gods and goddesses.

column3: someone who helps and supports someone else
column4: The US is one of Britain's staunchest allies.
column5: The two countries formed a strategic alliance to address common security concerns.
column6: The activist found a powerful ally in the media, amplifying her message to a wider audience.

column3: not friendly or willing to take part in things
column4: She seemed rather aloof when in fact she was just shy.
column5: Despite being at the party, she remained aloof and distant, preferring to observe from afar.
column6: The cat was known for its aloof demeanor, rarely seeking affection from its owners.

column3: a loud argument or disagreement
column4: According to witnesses, the altercation between the two men started inside the restaurant.
column5: The heated altercation between the neighbors over the property line escalated into a shouting match.
column6: The security guard intervened quickly to break up the altercation before it turned physical.

column3: height above sea level
column4: Mountain climbers use oxygen when they reach higher altitudes.
column5: As the plane ascended, the altitude increased, offering passengers a breathtaking view of the landscape below.
column6: The village's high altitude made it challenging for crops to grow, but the residents adapted by cultivating hardy varieties of plants.

column3: showing a wish to help or bring advantages to others, even if it results in disadvantage for yourself
column4: I doubt whether her motives for donating the money are altruistic - she's probably looking for publicity.
column5: Her altruistic actions, such as volunteering at the homeless shelter, inspired others to give back to their community.
column6: The philanthropist's altruistic donation funded the construction of a new school in an underserved area.

column3: taking part in an activity for pleasure, not as a job
column4: He was an amateur singer until the age of 40, when he turned professional.
column5: As an amateur photographer, he enjoyed capturing moments with his camera but had yet to master the technical aspects of the craft
column6: The band's performance was amateurish, lacking the polish and professionalism of seasoned musicians.

column3: having or expressing more than one possible meaning, sometimes intentionally
column4: His reply to my question was somewhat ambiguous.
column5: The instructions were ambiguous, leaving us uncertain about what to do next.
column6: His response to the question was deliberately ambiguous, causing confusion among the audience.

column3: a strong wish to achieve something
column4: He has already achieved his main ambition in life - to become wealthy.
column5: His ambition to become a successful entrepreneur drove him to work tirelessly on his startup.
column6: She had ambitious goals for her career, aiming to climb the corporate ladder and become a CEO.

column3: the state of having two opposing feelings at the same time, or being uncertain about how you feel
column4: Real social relationships are marked by ambiguity and ambivalence.
column5: She felt a sense of ambivalence about accepting the job offer, torn between the opportunity for career advancement and the comfort of her current position.
column6: Her ambivalence towards the idea of marriage reflected her uncertainty about committing to a lifelong partnership.

column3: walk or move at a slow, relaxed pace, can also be the plural noun of amble, a walk at relaxed pace
column4: She ambled down the street, stopping occasionally to look in the shop windows.
column5: The couple strolled hand in hand, taking leisurely ambles along the beach.
column6: The horse ambles along the trail, enjoying the peaceful surroundings of the countryside.

column3: To change or modify in any way for the better
column4: In line 20, men should be amended to people.
column5: The company decided to amend its policies to better address employee concerns.
column6: He promised to amend his behavior after realizing the impact of his actions on others.

column3: Lovable; lovely; pleasing
column4: The host greeted us with an amiable smile, making us feel instantly welcome in his home.
column5: The new coworker was friendly and amiable, quickly making friends with everyone in the office.
column6: Despite their differences, they maintained an amiable relationship, always willing to lend a helping hand to each other.

column3: Having no determinate form; of irregular; shapeless
column4: an amorphous mass of jelly
column5: The artist created an amorphous sculpture that seemed to defy conventional shapes and forms
column6: The concept was still amorphous, lacking clear direction or definition.

column3: more than enough
column4: You'll have ample opportunity to ask questions after the talk.
column5: The buffet offered ample choices, satisfying even the pickiest eaters.
column6: She had ample time to prepare for the exam, thanks to the extended study period.

column3: a word or phrase made by using the letters of another word or phrase in a different order
column4: Neat is an anagram of a net.
column5: She enjoyed solving anagrams as a brain-teaser during her free time.
column6: The word 'listen' is an anagram of 'silent', showcasing the playful nature of language.

column3: a comparison between things that have similar features, often used to help explain a principle or idea
column4: He drew an analogy between the brain and a vast computer.
column5: The teacher used the analogy of a garden to explain the concept of ecosystem to her students
column6: Drawing an analogy between life and a journey, he likened challenges to obstacles along the path.

column3: someone who wishes to destroy the existing government and laws
column4: He was a poet, an anarchist, and a vegan.
column5: The anarchist group protested against government policies, advocating for radical change.
column6: The anarchist's disdain for authority led to clashes with law enforcement.

column3: a short, often funny story, especially about something someone has done
column4: He told one or two amusing anecdotes about his years as a policeman.
column5: The speaker shared a personal anecdote to illustrate the importance of perseverance in achieving success.
column6: The book was filled with entertaining anecdotes about the author's adventures while traveling the world.

column3: strong worry or unhappiness, especially about personal problems
column4: All my children went through a period of late-adolescent angst.
column5: As a teenager, she experienced a lot of angst about her future career path.
column6: The protagonist of the novel grapples with existential angst as he searches for the meaning of life.

column3: extreme unhappiness caused by physical or mental suffering
column4: Somehow we deal with the anguish of serious illness.
column5: She felt a deep sense of anguish upon hearing the news of her friend's passing.
column6: The parents experienced anguish as they searched for their missing child, fearing the worst.

column3: strong dislike, opposition, or anger
column4: Of course we're competitive, but there's no personal animosity between us.
column5: There was a palpable animosity between the rival sports teams, evident in their fierce competition on the field.
column6: Despite their efforts to conceal it, there was an underlying animosity between the two coworkers, stemming from past conflicts.

column3: to destroy something completely so that nothing is left
column4: In the particle collision experiment, the high-energy beam annihilated with its antiparticle counterpart, producing a burst of gamma radiation.
column5: The army quickly annihilated the enemy forces, leaving no survivors on the battlefield.
column6: The hurricane's powerful winds and storm surge annihilated entire coastal communities, leaving behind widespread devastation.

column3: actively opposing or showing unfriendliness towards something or someone
column4: He's extremely antagonistic towards all critics.
column5: Their antagonistic relationship often resulted in heated arguments and bitter disagreements.
column6: The antagonist in the novel played a crucial role in driving the plot forward, challenging the protagonist at every turn

column3: a way of preventing or acting against something bad
column4: Regular exercise is the best antidote to tiredness and depression.
column5: The doctor administered an antidote to counteract the effects of the poison.
column6: Laughter is often regarded as the best antidote to stress and anxiety.

column3: a feeling of strong dislike, opposition, or anger
column4: Despite the deep antipathies between them, the two sides have managed to negotiate an agreement.
column5: There was mutual antipathy between the two rival gangs, leading to frequent clashes and violence.
column6: Her antipathy towards public speaking made it difficult for her to deliver presentations with confidence.

column3: old-fashioned or unsuitable for modern society
column4: Compared with modern satellite dishes, ordinary TV aerials look positively antiquated.
column5: The old mansion was filled with antiquated furniture and d cor, giving it a sense of timeless charm.
column6: The company's antiquated computer systems were in desperate need of an upgrade to keep pace with modern technology.

column3: an object that was created a very long time ago
column4: Under Greek law, all antiquities that are discovered in Greece belong to the government.
column5: The archaeologist discovered ancient artifacts dating back to antiquity during the excavation.
column6: The historian studied the literature of antiquity to gain insights into the culture and society of the past.

column3: the exact opposite
column4: He is the exact antithesis of what I find attractive in men.
column5: His outgoing personality is the antithesis of her shy demeanor.
column6: The artist's latest collection represents the antithesis of traditional painting techniques, embracing abstract forms and bold colors.

column3: worried and nervous
column4: The drought has made farmers anxious about the harvest.
column5: She felt anxious about the upcoming job interview, worried about whether she would perform well.
column6: The dog became anxious during thunderstorms, seeking comfort from its owner.

column3: showing no interest or energy and unwilling to take action, especially over something important
column4: Young people today are so apathetic about politics.
column5: The students seemed apathetic about the upcoming class project, showing little interest in participating.
column6: The politician's apathetic response to the pressing issues facing the community disappointed many of his constituents.

column3: a short clever saying that is intended to express a general truth
column4: Oscar Wilde was famous for such aphorisms as Experience is the name everyone gives to their mistakes.
column5: The aphorism 'actions speak louder than words' emphasizes the importance of deeds over mere promises.
column6: The writer often begins her essays with an aphorism to capture the reader's attention and convey a key message.

column3: extremely and obviously angry
column4: His apoplectic reaction to the news left everyone in the room stunned, as he shouted and gestured wildly.
column5: The manager's apoplectic outburst over the team's poor performance was both unexpected and unsettling.
column6: The chef's face turned apoplectic when he discovered the kitchen had run out of essential ingredients just before the dinner service.

column3: to prevent further disagreement or fighting by letting the opposing side have something that they want
column4: They were given a small pay rise but this was not enough to appease them.
column5: The manager tried to appease the angry customer by offering a full refund and a sincere apology.
column6: The government attempted to appease protesters by promising to address their grievances through policy reforms.

column3: worry about the future, or a fear that something unpleasant is going to happen
column4: It's normal to feel a little apprehension before starting a new school.
column5: She felt a sense of apprehension before her first day at a new job, unsure of what to expect.
column6: The prisoner's apprehension grew as he awaited the verdict of the jury, fearing the worst.

column3: to inform or notify someone of something
column4: Please apprise me of any changes to the schedule as soon as possible.
column5: The manager apprised the team of the new project requirements during the morning meeting.
column6: The teacher apprised the students of the upcoming exam schedule.

column3: to officially agree to or accept something, often after considering it carefully or after it meets certain criteria
column4: The city council is expected to approve the construction of a new park in the downtown area.
column5: The principal approved the students' request to organize a charity event at the school.
column6: The regulatory agency refused to approve the use of the new drug until further safety tests were conducted.

column3: Depending on will or discretion; not governed by any fixed rules
column4: The selection process seemed arbitrary, with no clear criteria for choosing the winner.
column5: The teacher's grading system appeared arbitrary, leaving students confused about how their work was evaluated.
column6: The company's decision to lay off employees felt arbitrary, as many felt it was based on favoritism rather than performance.

column3: Of or characterized by antiquity or archaism
column4: The ancient manuscript was written in archaic language, making it difficult to decipher.
column5: The customs of the tribe seemed archaic to outsiders, reflecting traditions passed down for generations.
column6: The building's architecture was considered archaic compared to the sleek, modern designs of neighboring structures.
